title: Copperplate lower-case alphabet, a to z
subtitle: The English roundhand lower-case letters with pressure-and-release stroke order
excerpt: Copperplate is a pointed-nib tradition: the thick downstrokes come from pressing the tines apart; the hairline upstrokes come from releasing them. This tutorial teaches the complete lower-case alphabet with stroke order, shade placement, and the key proportion rules.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866), plus the Spencer Brothers copybooks (various editions, 1868 onward). Both are in the public domain. Edward Cocker, The Pen's Triumph and related penmanship copybooks (17th century) document the English roundhand that Spencerian grew from. Internet Archive collections.

content.text: In
Copperplate

, every letter is built from three basic shapes: the oval (the spine of a, d, g, q, and the loop in p and b), the under-turn (the base of n, m, and u), and the over-turn (the arch of h, k, m, and y). The
shades
fall only on the downstrokes; the
entry strokes
are always hairlines. The pen is held in an oblique holder so the nib points at roughly 55 degrees from the baseline, aligning with the slope of the script.
What you need

items.name: Pointed nib (Nikko G for beginners, stiff enough to control)
Oblique pen holder (right-handed, with flange set for 55-degree slope)
Iron-gall ink or calligraphy gouache thinned to ink consistency
Hot-press watercolour paper, 200 gsm, or smooth cartridge 130 gsm
Gum sandarac (optional, dusted on paper surface to reduce bleed)

content.text: Guide lines and slope

Rule five parallel lines per writing line: baseline, x-height (2.5 mm above baseline for a fine Nikko G), descender line (3 mm below baseline), ascender line (4 mm above baseline), and a slope guideline at 55 degrees from the baseline. The 55-degree slope is the visual signature of classic English roundhand. Reduce to 52 degrees for a less formal effect.
The oval and the basic strokes

Practise the oval before any letters. Entry at the 1 o'clock position, hairline upstroke to the top of the oval, then press and pull down-left in a smooth shade to the baseline, and curve back up in a hairline to close at the entry point. The oval should be narrow (roughly one-third as wide as it is tall), slope at 55 degrees, and have a single even shade on its left curve.
Letter groups and stroke order
Oval group: a, d, g, q

a is three strokes. Stroke 1: enter from the baseline at the right side of the oval, hairline up to the top-right, then shade down-left to the baseline, closing into a small foot. This is the oval. Stroke 2: hairline upstroke from the baseline at the right of the oval, curving up to the x-height. Stroke 3: shade downstroke from the x-height to the baseline with a right-side exit foot. d extends the same a structure with a tall ascender on stroke 3, rising to the ascender line before coming back to the baseline.
Under-turn group: i, t, u, w, n, m

i is the basic under-turn unit. Entry hairline from the baseline, curving up to the x-height, then shade downstroke from x-height to the baseline, curving right at the base into the exit hairline. The shade falls on the full left curve of the downstroke. n is two under-turn units joined at the x-height: the shade of the first and the entry of the second share the same top curve. m is three units.
Over-turn group: h, k, b, p, f

h starts with a tall ascender stroke (shade from the ascender line to the baseline, exiting with the foot hairline), then branches from the top of the shade at two-thirds of the x-height with an arch that mirrors the under-turn of n. The arch goes over the top and comes down in a shade to the baseline. k uses the same ascender as h but adds a small loop at mid-x-height and a diagonal exit.
Descender group: g, j, p, q, y, z

g uses the oval as its body (same as a), then the shade downstroke extends below the baseline to the descender line, where it loops to the right and back up in a hairline, returning to the baseline exit point. The descender loop should be about the same width as the oval.
Common mistakes

attrs.intro: Problems that appear in the first weeks of pointed-nib practice.

items.fix: Practise the entry-shade-exit sequence in isolation without any letter: enter hairline, press to shade at the top, pull down in shade, release to hairline. The shade only lives on the downstroke.
items.cause: Pressure applied on the wrong stroke. In Copperplate, pressure opens the tines on the downstroke only.
items.symptom: Shades are on the wrong strokes (upstrokes are thick, downstrokes are thin)

items.fix: Load less ink. Begin each shade with a very gentle pressure increase, not a sudden press.
items.cause: Too much ink loaded, or nib pressed down too abruptly rather than gradually.
items.symptom: Ink floods the paper at the start of each shade

items.fix: Check the flange angle in the oblique holder (should angle the nib at 55 degrees from the writing line). Rule slope guidelines and follow them consciously.
items.cause: Pen not angled correctly in the oblique holder, or slope line not being followed.
items.symptom: Ovals are round rather than narrow and sloped

attrs.heading: Common errors in Copperplate lower case
